title: Full Product Hostile Exploratory Audit
goal: Audit the explorer as a skeptical, pessimistic software tester would: inventory all visible features, push combined scenarios, inspect pane-level screenshots, and judge the product on readability, coherence, truthfulness, and stability rather than on click success alone.
intent: Behave like a demanding user who assumes the app is wrong until the evidence says otherwise. Check every visible feature, ask whether it is supposed to look and behave this way, and collect reproducible evidence for both strengths and defects.
reviewQuestions: If I forget the implementation history and only judge the product in front of me, would I trust it?
After each interaction, is the new state obvious and is the unchanged state still coherent?
Does every visible control justify its existence with a clear, readable outcome?
Do any labels, layouts, or copy blocks create avoidable doubt, friction, or false confidence?
reminders: Do not treat a visible widget as a working feature. Inspect what it changes, what it fails to change, and what collateral inconsistency it leaves behind.
Use screenshots as evidence. If a screenshot is too broad to judge, capture a narrower one instead of guessing.
Look around the target area. Neighboring spacing, contrast, stale text, and overflow are part of the bug surface.
When something feels merely acceptable, ask what a more careful design would do instead.
baseline: Run the stable acceptance suite first.
Use focused screenshots for panes and long surfaces; full-page captures alone hide defects.
Record findings in user-facing product language first, technical detail second.

steps.title: Iteration 01: landing inventory and first-trust check
steps.intent: List the visible product surfaces and judge whether the first viewport establishes trust or already creates doubt.
steps.focus: Whole app-shell hierarchy at first load.
lookAround: Does the page explain itself quickly, or does it require interpretation before use?
Is the first selected problem, recommendation area, and left rail relationship visually sane?
Do any labels sound vague, inflated, or mechanically generated?
reviewQuestions: Would an experienced engineer trust this page after three seconds?
steps.observation: Iteration 01 is a shallow pass intended to map the visible product and catch first-order trust failures.

steps.title: Iteration 02: left rail, advanced filters, and control fit
steps.intent: Audit the orientation rail as navigation and filter surface rather than as decoration.
steps.focus: Sidebar routes, guides, metrics, and advanced filter disclosure.
lookAround: Does the left rail read as stable orientation or as a crowded collection of small boxes?
When advanced filters open, do the chips still look controllable and readable?
Do metrics and topic links earn their space, or are they just there because space existed?
reviewQuestions: Is the left rail genuinely useful, or just tolerated?
steps.observation: Iteration 02 deepens the audit into the lowest-priority surface, where weak spacing and weak copy often hide.

steps.title: Iteration 03: orientation row and recommendation quality
steps.intent: Judge whether the recommendation layer makes confident decisions or merely rearranges inventory.
steps.focus: Overview row, recommendation cards, visible-slice summary, and guide strip.
lookAround: Do the recommendation cards say why now, or do they just restate metadata?
Does the guide strip look intentionally attached to the current slice?
Does the active-filter bar stay readable when nothing special is active?
reviewQuestions: Does this section reduce decision effort or add more things to parse?
steps.observation: Iteration 03 checks whether the overview layer functions as editorial guidance instead of decorative segmentation.

steps.title: Iteration 04: roadmap legibility and operational linkage
steps.intent: Treat the roadmap as a navigation instrument and inspect whether it deserves the space it occupies.
steps.focus: Roadmap labels, nodes, stage chips, and feedback into the workspace.
lookAround: Are the lane labels, chips, and node clusters readable without strain?
Does the map visibly change the rest of the workspace in a way a human would understand?
Does the map still feel integrated rather than inserted?
reviewQuestions: Would I ever choose the roadmap as my way into the catalog?
steps.observation: Iteration 04 checks the first major navigation surface after the opening layout.

steps.title: Iteration 05: catalog scan, compact mode, and selected-state clarity
steps.intent: Audit the catalog as a repeated-use browsing surface where subtle hierarchy mistakes accumulate quickly.
steps.focus: Catalog row design, compact mode, active state, and bottom-of-list behavior.
lookAround: Is the active row unmistakable in compact mode too?
Do the rows remain scannable, or does compact mode collapse them into near-duplicates?
Does scrolling the catalog preserve a stable relationship with the detail pane?
reviewQuestions: Would I want to browse twenty rows like this?
steps.observation: Iteration 05 focuses on the product's most repetitive surface, where weak row design becomes expensive.

steps.title: Iteration 06: search, sorting, and narrow result states
steps.intent: Probe whether reduced or reordered states still look intentional instead of accidentally sparse.
steps.focus: Search for a single result, sort changes, and active filter summaries.
lookAround: When the list shrinks, does the page still look designed rather than broken?
Does the active-filter summary explain the state well enough?
Does sorting tell the truth about what the user is seeing?
reviewQuestions: Would a user understand why this one item is here and what caused that state?
steps.observation: Iteration 06 is intentionally narrow because sparse states often reveal weak empty-space handling and weak explanations.

steps.title: Iteration 07: pinning, solving, notes, and reload persistence
steps.intent: Combine personal-state features and check whether the app remains internally honest after reload.
steps.focus: Selected problem controls, saved note, persistent badges, and post-reload consistency.

actions.value: Hostile audit note: verify solved + pinned + note survive.

lookAround: Do solved and pinned states remain obvious without becoming noisy?
After reload, is any copy stale or contradictory?
Does the note box still look intentionally placed once it contains real text?
reviewQuestions: Does state persistence feel reliable, not just technically present?
steps.observation: Iteration 07 combines the app's personal-state features because integration bugs hide there, not in isolated clicks.

steps.title: Iteration 08: widened detail pane and lower-section reading quality
steps.intent: Treat the detail pane as an editorial reading surface and inspect whether the lower sections are actually readable.
steps.focus: Widened reading mode, lower detail sections, and scroll behavior.
lookAround: Does widening materially improve reading comfort?
Do lower sections maintain spacing and hierarchy, or do they degrade into a stack of pale blocks?
Does scrolling this pane feel stable and separate from the catalog?
reviewQuestions: Would I willingly read the whole detail pane in this mode?
steps.observation: Iteration 08 moves below the fold because visual quality often drops once the top summary is out of view.

steps.title: Iteration 09: inline spoiler layer and focused solution workspace
steps.intent: Audit both solution surfaces as study tools, not just embedded code containers.
steps.focus: Inline spoiler affordance, attribution clarity, reviewed code readability, and modal comparison mode.
lookAround: Does the inline spoiler still protect the default decision-making mode?
Is attribution unmistakable once code is revealed?
Does the modal workspace make comparison easier, or just louder?
reviewQuestions: Would I study from these surfaces instead of leaving the site?
steps.observation: Iteration 09 hits the deepest reading surfaces where credibility and code presentation matter most.

steps.title: Iteration 10: topic guide quality and route back into work
steps.intent: Leave the main explorer, inspect the topic-guide page as a real learning surface, and judge whether the return path preserves trust.
steps.focus: Topic page readability, linked practice list, and explorer return affordance.
lookAround: Does the topic page feel like an intentional companion product or a secondary dump of blocks?
Are the practice links, review checklist, and back-to-explorer action visually balanced?
Would I know how to continue from here without friction?
reviewQuestions: Does the topic-guide layer deserve to exist as a first-class part of the product?
steps.observation: Iteration 10 closes the audit by checking whether the site still feels coherent after leaving the main workspace.
